insight: When two independent advisor lenses (e.g., Simplicity + Organizational) converge on the same simplification, treat it as strong signal and take it. In this session, both flagged R22 per-file persist-back routing as over-engineered for v1; collapsing it to a single 'save whole tree to active profile' choice cut estimated dev time ~40% with no loss of day-one user value. Same-direction signal across different lenses is much stronger than within-lens elaboration.

Atomic directory replacement uses the pending/prior rename pattern: (a) populate .pending/, (b) rename existing canonical to .prior/, (c) rename .pending/ to canonical. Recovery is symmetric — if .prior/ exists at startup, rename it back; if only .pending/ exists, discard. This is mechanically simpler than 'try operation, catch errors, rollback' logic because rename() is the only atomic primitive POSIX gives you for directories. Pair with a lockfile (PID + timestamp, kill -0 staleness check) for concurrency, and use temp-file + rename for the state file so readers never see torn writes. These three primitives (pending/prior, lockfile, atomic state write) together cover the bulk of crash-recovery requirements for FS-mutating CLIs.

Always include schemaVersion in any state/config file the tool writes and reads, from day one. Treat unparseable or schema-mismatched files as a benign degraded state (e.g., NoActive), never a fatal error. Cost is one field; benefit is forward-compatibility forever. Cross-checked by both interface designer and STPA control structure analyst in this session as a non-negotiable. The pattern: { schemaVersion: 1, ...rest }; readers validate; on mismatch, log a warning and behave as if the file didn't exist.

ResolvedPlan schema lock pattern: when an epic mandates a contract freeze (per fitness function), build the contract doc co-located with code (docs/specs/contracts/<contract>.md) listing schemaVersion, invariants, error-trigger rules, and worked examples. Cite the EARS subset that constrains each field and ensure tests assert each invariant. Add forward-compat clauses up front (additive optional fields don't bump version).

When implementing per-type merge with strategy pattern: keep the strategy function signature byte-pure ((relPath, ContributorBytes[]) => StrategyResult). Move all IO into a thin orchestrator with an injectable read() so unit tests of strategies are sync, fast, and disk-free, while integration tests round-trip through the real fs via the resolver.

JSON.parse produces __proto__ as own enumerable property; deep-merge over a JSON object must use Object.defineProperty (not direct assignment) to preserve __proto__ as a literal data key — otherwise the prototype setter elides it from JSON.stringify output, causing silent data loss

When implementing pending/prior atomic-rename protocol on Node.js, use per-attempt unique tmp file paths (e.g. .lock.<pid>.<nonce>.tmp) rather than a shared canonical .lock.tmp. Two concurrent acquirers racing on a shared tmp path can produce a verify-read fall-through where the loser's verify sees a stale PID and silently proceeds. Pair with a bounded retry loop (3 attempts) when verify-read shows a dead-PID lock — re-claim by retrying acquire from scratch, only escalate to LockHeldError on a live racer. This is the same lockfile pattern used by git.

On Windows, process.kill(pid, 0) is an unreliable liveness check for cross-process locks: PID recycling is fast and cross-session/cross-user PIDs return ambiguous results (true for unknown reasons). Supplement kill(0) with a max-age threshold (e.g. 1 hour) on win32 only — if the lock timestamp is older than the threshold, treat as stale even when kill(0) returns success. POSIX kill(0) semantics are unambiguous (ESRCH = definitely dead) so this guard is platform-conditional.

Two-tier fingerprint comparison must split metadata walk from content hashing. Walk live tree once for stat() (size+mtime) only; for files whose metadata diverges from the recorded fingerprint, lazily read+hash that single file. Eagerly hashing the entire tree in fingerprintTree() then short-circuiting in compareFingerprint() does the IO anyway and negates the fast-path's purpose. The savings matter at 1000+ files where hashing dominates the drift-check latency budget.

Pure decision functions are the right granularity for state machines whose IO half is owned by another epic. E4's gate decision (decideGate) is a pure function over (DriftReport, mode, --on-drift= flag) returning {kind: 'no-drift'|'auto'|'prompt', choice}. The interactive prompt itself lives in E5. This split lets E4 own the invariant tests (hard-block, no infinite block in non-interactive) while E5 owns IO. Same pattern applies wherever a 'must prompt the user' decision can be cleanly separated from the prompt itself.

Fail-open invariants in shell-hook entry points must wrap stderr writes in try/catch. process.stderr.write can throw EPIPE synchronously when the parent (e.g. git pre-commit driver) closes the pipe. If the throw escapes, the function rejects and process.exit(0) never runs — the hook ends up with a non-zero exit despite the entire body being designed to never block. Pattern: outer try/catch around the whole side-effect block + inner try/catch per write that breaks out cleanly on first failure. Lose the message, never block the commit.

When a degraded-state warning code differentiates 'normal absence' from 'actually broken', surface only the broken cases in user-facing entry points like pre-commit hooks. E4's StateReadWarning has Missing|ParseError|SchemaMismatch; Missing is the normal fresh-project case (no init has run yet) and should NOT emit a warning on every commit. ParseError/SchemaMismatch indicate genuine corruption and SHOULD warn. Suppressing Missing keeps the hook invisible in the happy path while still surfacing the cases users need to know about.

Use default import (`import process from 'node:process'`) NOT namespace import (`import * as process from 'node:process'`). The namespace form exposes internals like `_events` and `_eventsCount` but loses EventEmitter prototype methods (`process.on`) and TTY props (`process.stdin.isTTY`). Tests with signal handlers disabled work fine because they never call `.on`; production fails at runtime when bin tries to register SIGINT handler with 'process.on is not a function', AND TTY detection silently degrades to `undefined && undefined === false` so non-interactive code paths trigger in real terminals. Reproduce: `node -e "import('node:process').then(p => console.log(typeof p.on))"` prints 'undefined'.

When testing modules that register process signal handlers (e.g. lock cleanup), make the signalHandlers opt REQUIRED at every layer of the call chain — never optional with a default-true. The default-true form lets a test forget to pass false, causing a vitest worker error like 'Cannot redefine property: _eventsCount' at handler-registration time, which is confusing because it points at the lock module, not the test. Required-boolean forces every caller (bin always true, tests always false) to be explicit. The slight extra typing is worth it: omitting the param becomes a type error caught at test-write time, not a runtime crash inside framework code.

Pattern for end-to-end SIGINT/SIGTERM cleanup tests: write a tiny ESM helper script (.mjs, no compilation step) that invokes the production code path (e.g. `await withLock(paths, async () => { process.stdout.write('READY\n'); await heartbeat(); })`), then have the test spawn it, wait for the 'READY' marker on stdout, send the signal, and assert (a) exit code === 128 + signo (SIGINT=130, SIGTERM=143) and (b) the on-disk artifact is gone. Critical detail: `await new Promise(() => {})` exits immediately on Node 20+ with 'Warning: Detected unsettled top-level await'. Pair the unsettled promise with a long-period `setInterval(() => {}, 60_000)` to keep the event loop alive so the signal can fire. Without the heartbeat, Node bails before any signal can be delivered.

Enforce CLI --json mode invariants once in the OutputChannel, not in every command. Pattern: createOutput({json}) returns {print, json, warn, error}; print() and warn() are NO-OPs in json mode while error() always writes and json() always emits. Command code branches on `output.jsonMode` to choose payload SHAPE (human strings vs structured payload) but never branches on whether to write — the channel is a hard guard. This collapses the 'silence human output' epic invariant into one place that's impossible to bypass from a command file. Counter-pattern (rejected): each command checking `if (jsonMode) { json(...) } else { print(...) }` for every line — leaks a stray print() and you've broken the contract.

CLI exit-code policy: centralise the error→code mapping in one exitCodeFor(err) function and one CliUserError class that carries its own exitCode. Command handlers never call process.exit and never invent their own exit-code arithmetic — they throw, and a single catch in bin.ts maps to a code via exitCodeFor. Concrete map for a CLI sitting on a typed error hierarchy: ResolverError subclasses + LockHeldError → 3 (conflict/missing class), MergeError → 2 (runtime IO drift), CliNotImplementedError → 2 (system-class stub for cross-epic deferral), unknown → 2, CliUserError → its own (typically 1). The matrix becomes a small unit-test table that locks the contract; any new error class is one switch arm to register.

Non-TTY hard-block UX pattern (paired with E4's pure decideGate lesson L29affb99): when a CLI session is non-interactive AND a decision flag was not pre-supplied, exit 1 IMMEDIATELY with a message that names the flag the user should add (e.g. 'pass --on-drift=discard|persist|abort'). Never block waiting on stdin — CI scripts hang forever. Never auto-pick a default — silently destructive. The pattern is: detect non-TTY in the bin entry (`process.stdin.isTTY && process.stdout.isTTY`), pass mode='non-interactive' down, and have the orchestrator throw CliUserError with the literal flag name in the message. Test by spawning the bin against a piped stdin () and asserting exit code AND the message contains the flag string.

Cross-epic imports must always go through the epic's barrel (./<epic>/index.js), even when the importing module lives in the same monorepo. Direct submodule imports (../resolver/paths.js → buildPaths) silently couple the consumer to the producer's internal layout — a refactor inside the producer epic breaks consumers without any type signal. If a symbol the consumer needs isn't on the barrel, ADD it (and document why on the public surface) rather than reach in. Concrete check: grep imports for direct paths into peer-epic submodules; route through barrels and add the missing exports. This was a P1 architecture finding for E5 because two CLI commands imported buildPaths and isValidProfileName directly from src/resolver/paths.js.

Node 20 readline.promises.question() does NOT reject when the input stream closes (Ctrl-D / pipe end) — it just hangs. To enforce an EOF contract, wire an AbortController to rl.once('close', () => ac.abort()) and pass {signal} into question(). The signal-aware path rejects with AbortError on close; without it, your test will time out and your CLI prompt will hang.

When a verb in a multi-verb CLI has a 'fail-open / soft-degrade' edge case (e.g. hook install in a non-git project) AND a 'fail-loud / surface error' edge case, expose both via a single Boolean opt like `allowSkip`. The two callers (init flow vs explicit `hook install` verb) get the right semantics by passing different values, and the underlying primitive stays a single function with one error-path. Pattern observed in claude-code-profiles E6: init passes allowSkip=true so a non-git bootstrap doesn't abort, while `hook install` passes false so the user sees the error.

E7 binary-level integration tests: spawn the built dist/cli/bin.js subprocess but use in-process resolve()/merge()/materialize() to set up fixture state. Setting up via in-process calls is pragmatic and doesn't undermine the gate, since the assertion target is post-spawn behavior. The lock primitive's exit-3 contract holds at unit level but under heavy concurrent contention some losers exit code 2 due to a secondary materialize race — the binary-surface gate must tolerate the mixed exit codes while asserting at least one CONFLICT path (3) traversal per race.

Integration test of S16 (crash mid-materialize recovery via .prior/ rename-back) shows that the binary-surface contract differs from the unit-level contract: materialize() reconciles internally, but the swap orchestrator's drift detection runs BEFORE materialize() so a bare 'use' aborts at the drift gate. The user can recover via --on-drift=discard. The deeper fix is to call reconcileMaterialize at the start of swap orchestrator before drift detection.

When implementing a help-text audit across N CLI verbs, lock the format with an integration test that asserts every verb's --help contains the standard sections (USAGE/DESCRIPTION/EXAMPLES/EXIT CODES). One snapshot test per section is more durable than a single full-text snapshot — the latter forces the test to update on every wording tweak, the former only fails on actual structural drift.

For colour/glyph helpers in Node CLIs without a chalk dependency, derive every decision from explicit options (isTty, platform, noColor) rather than reading process.stdout.isTTY directly inside the helper. Pure helpers are deterministic in tests, reproducible across CI hosts, and let one helper handle both colour-on-stderr and colour-on-stdout without duplicating logic.

When designing 'managed section' markers inside markdown files, use versioned HTML comments without content hash: <!-- claude-profiles:v1:begin --> ... <!-- claude-profiles:v1:end -->. HTML comments are invisible in all markdown renderers (GitHub, VS Code, pandoc). Versioning enables forward migration. Namespace prefix prevents tool collision. NO content hash because users will reflow surrounding prose, and hash-mismatch warnings train them to ignore the tool. Drift detection belongs in state machinery, not the marker. Add a self-documenting second line ('<!-- Managed block. Regenerate: ... -->') so the next human knows what they're looking at.

When fixing a bug that changes the trigger condition for an existing check, audit the EXISTING tests that exercised that check — they may have been silently passing for the wrong reason and will now legitimately fail. Pre-existing tests can encode bugs. Cw6 P1-A example: validate fired markers-check on every active profile, but test fixtures HAD no projectRoot contributor — so tests passed (exit 1 because markers absent). After gating the check on contributor presence, those tests started exiting 0. The fix was to update the test fixtures to actually have a projectRoot contributor (matching what the test was conceptually trying to test), not to revert the bug fix.

State schema migration strategy that worked for cw6: additive-optional fields without schema version bump. New optional field 'rootClaudeMdSection' on StateFile. Legacy state.json files (no key) load and treat the field as null. The discipline: any FUTURE field addition that changes COMPARISON SEMANTICS (e.g., a new hash-algorithm field) MUST trigger a schema version bump and explicit migration; pure additive fields with null-default behavior do not. Document this constraint in code comments next to the schema definition so future contributors don't accidentally add a semantic-changing field as optional.

When the implementation of a spec requirement can't fully achieve the spec's stated invariant, UPDATE THE SPEC to honestly describe the actual behavior — don't ship code that overstates its guarantees. Cw6 R45 example: spec said 'atomic across destinations' but the .claude/ rename swap commits before the projectRoot section splice. Reality: pre-flight failure = atomic abort (zero writes); mid-write splice IO failure post-step-c = recoverable on retry, NOT atomic. The fix was a 3-place spec update (R45 split, lifecycle table, migration guide) — not new rollback code. Honest spec is better than aspirational spec. Reviewer (Codex) caught the gap as P1.

When adding a write path that produces a new state field on every successful run, also handle the SWITCH-TO-NO-CONTRIBUTOR case explicitly. Cw6 P1-B: switching from a profile that contributed to project-root CLAUDE.md to one that didn't left the OLD profile's section bytes between the markers in the materialized file — Claude Code would still read them as active instructions. The fix: when rootMerged.length === 0 AND prior state had non-null rootClaudeMdSection AND markers are present, splice an EMPTY section (not skip the splice). Key invariant: 'state field is non-null' means 'there is content actively managed', so transitioning to no-contributor must clear the live content too. Otherwise stale content leaks across profile switches.

When padding columns in a CLI text table where cells may contain ANSI SGR escapes (bold/colour), measure VISIBLE length (escapes stripped) — not String.length. A bolded 4-char name has byte length ~13 but visible width 4, so naive padEnd over-pads neighbours. Strip escapes via /\x1b\[[0-9;]*m/g before measuring and pad by the resulting delta.

captureOutput(false) in tests/cli/helpers/output-sink.ts builds a non-TTY channel, and command code that reads process.stdout.isTTY directly (list.ts, status.ts) sees isTty=false in vitest runs. ANSI-active behaviors (bold, colour) therefore never fire in command-level tests. To regression-test the ANSI/TTY path, unit-test the lower-level renderer (renderTable, etc.) by passing escape sequences directly — don't try to fake isTTY through the command harness.

When CLI commands need 'did you mean' suggestions for typoed names, pre-flight pattern: (1) validate name format up-front via shared assertValidProfileName, (2) catch MissingProfileError after resolve, rebuild it with .suggestions populated. Keep error.code stable so machines key off it; only enrich .message. Use Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 (catches transposition/single-edit without surfacing unrelated 1-letter profiles). Cap at 3 matches sorted by distance then lex.

Integration tests via runCli execute the BUILT dist/cli/bin.js — pnpm build is required between source changes and integration assertions. Ergo: 'pnpm test' alone won't catch CLI-binary regressions if source changed since the last build. Sequence is build, then test (or rely on ensureBuilt() to fail loudly on a stale dist).

When CLI commands branch on jsonMode, build the Style helper inside the human-output branch (or behind a builder closure) — never unconditionally above the guard. Otherwise --json invocations read process.stdout.isTTY/process.platform/process.env they will never use, and the inconsistency makes future JSON-mode additions land with the wrong shape. Pattern: `function buildStyle() { return createStyle({...}); }` called only inside style-using branches.

When createStyle (or any pure helper) takes pre-resolved inputs that production callers always derive the same way, give the helper a sibling resolveX() function (e.g. resolveNoColor(globalNoColor, env)). Otherwise every call site duplicates 'global.flag || process.env.X !== undefined' and a future tweak (e.g. CLICOLOR_FORCE) requires editing N sites. The resolver also gives tests a single seam to mock env without monkey-patching globals.

When a polish-epic test pins broken behaviour with an explicit reference to the bug-fix issue (e.g. 'tracked as ch5'), assume the test will need updating once the fix lands — don't chase the green by reverting the fix. Look for matching bd issue references in test comments before declaring a regression.

When calling a unified-diff renderer (signature: a → '-' lines, b → '+' lines) inside a CLI command that already has its own '+'/'-' sigil convention, ALIGN the call site to the CLI's existing convention rather than adopting git's '<old> <new>' default. claude-profiles diff a b uses '+ only-a.md' (in a, not in b) for its file-level sigils — so renderUnifiedDiff(b, a) is the correct call (a-side becomes +, b-side becomes -). External reviewers without that context will flag this as a 'bug' because they expect git semantics. Add a clear comment at the call site naming the convention to short-circuit future reviews.

When adding TTY-dependent features to a CLI, plumb isTty through the OutputChannel (a testable injection point) rather than reading process.stdout.isTTY in each command. This lets snapshot tests pin colour-on rendering deterministically without monkey-patching the global stdout. Pattern: createOutput({ isTty: opts.isTty ?? Boolean(process.stdout.isTTY) }); commands read opts.output.isTty.

Pre-swap dry-run summaries should walk the live tree once and dispatch via callback (onPlanSummary). Compute in the orchestrator (runSwap), pass to the prompt for interactive context, surface as a single stderr line via the phase channel for human mode (auto-silenced under --json/--quiet), and include the structured payload on SwapResult so --json consumers see the same data without re-walking. Avoids three-walk waste and keeps the phase silencing in one place.

Acceptance criterion 'tests fail when reverting code paths' is best verified by stashing the working tree, running tests, then popping. The stash will surprise you by reverting EVERY tracked source file change, not just the one you targeted; copy the file to /tmp first and sed-revert in place. Why: avoids losing in-progress source mods you haven't yet committed.

Polish loop reviewer fleet (claude-sonnet, claude-opus, gemini) consistently produces empty/garbage reports — appears to be a stdin-piping bug in the generated polish-loop.sh script (reviewers respond with 'Ready to help. What are you working on?' or 'No input provided via stdin'). The polish architect detects empty reports and recovers gracefully by exercising the CLI directly + reading bd followups. So polish loops still produce useful epics, but the reviewer audit phase is non-functional. Worth fixing in ca polish script generation: verify the heredoc/stdin redirection. Symptom: reviewer reports are 0–135 bytes; gemini.stderr says 'No input provided via stdin'.

Renaming a CLI binary in an npm package is a multi-coordinate change, not a single edit. Audit every place the CLI writes its own name into user-controlled files BEFORE committing to a decomposition: (1) package.json bin map, (2) in-CLI text (help/errors/progress hints), (3) gitignore section header (e.g. `# Added by <name>`), (4) CLAUDE.md or other managed-block markers (e.g. `<!-- <name>:v1:begin -->`), (5) pre-commit/git hook scripts (e.g. `command -v <name>`). Each of these creates a forced choice at rename time: rename in lockstep, freeze as an opaque magic string, or provide back-compat reads. Concrete trigger from this repo: c3p rename — initial AskUserQuestion was 'replace bin only'; second question surfaced the other four coordinates and changed scope materially. Apply: when planning a CLI rename, run `grep -rn '<old-name>' src/` BEFORE asking the user about rename policy, and surface every category of hit as an explicit policy decision.

Grep-based verification of a CLI rename has a substring-collision blind spot. A naive filter like `grep <old-name> | grep -v <package-name>` accidentally hides standalone hits on lines that ALSO contain the package name as a prefix (e.g. bd issue IDs of the form `<package>-<id>`). Concrete trigger: c3p rename — `tests/cli/integration/completions.test.ts:2` had a JSDoc 'Integration tests for `claude-profiles completions <shell>` (claude-code-profiles-0zn).' The verification grep `grep claude-profiles | grep -v claude-code-profiles` hid this line because the SAME line contained the bd ID. The straggler was caught by a reviewer agent only because it read the diff directly. Apply: for grep verification of a rename, do not filter by line-contains; instead substring-substitute the kept tokens BEFORE matching. Robust pattern: `grep <old-name> path | sed 's|<keep-prefix-1>[a-z0-9-]*||g; s|<keep-prefix-2>||g' | grep <old-name>`. Or use word boundaries with awk/perl. NEVER trust a single line-level `grep -v` filter to validate a CLI rename.

Hard renames (no back-compat shim) are valid in pre-1.0 alpha CLIs, but the CHANGELOG must enumerate every manual upgrade step a user takes — one omission per coordinate creates a silent post-upgrade bug. Concrete trigger: c3p rename — five surfaces moved (bin, in-CLI text, gitignore header, CLAUDE.md markers, pre-commit hook). Each leaves user-repo state that is no longer recognized after upgrade. The CHANGELOG explicitly documents: (1) install new bin, (2) manually delete legacy gitignore section, (3) manually delete legacy markers, (4) re-run `init` for fresh markers/header, (5) re-run `hook install --force` to overwrite the legacy hook, (6) re-source completions for the new name. Skipping any one leaves a silent failure mode (e.g. without step 5, pre-commit fails open silently because `command -v <old-name>` returns non-zero). Apply: when shipping a hard rename in a pre-1.0 CLI, write the CHANGELOG migration steps by walking the same coordinate list used for the rename audit, in order. Test the upgrade path by running each step on a fixture project before merging.

Acceptance criteria that gate on grep counts ("stdout contains zero <token> substrings") need explicit exemption clauses for namespace overlap. AC text like 'AC-3: stdout contains zero `claude-profiles` substrings' looked precise but failed verification because the implementation correctly retained references to `.claude-profiles/` (the on-disk store directory, intentionally NOT renamed) and `claude-code-config-profiles` (the npm package name, intentionally NOT renamed). The criterion verified manually only after applying the same exemption filters by hand. Apply: when writing AC for grep-based gates during a rename, explicitly list exempt prefixes inside the criterion text (e.g. 'zero standalone `claude-profiles` tokens; substrings of `.claude-profiles/` and `claude-code-config-profiles` are exempt'). Better: encode the exemption as a sed-substitute step in the verification command and assert the post-substitute count is zero. The literal-zero criterion is the right INTENT but the wrong WORDING for a multi-meaning rename.

When N implementer agents work in parallel on the same workspace, intermediate test/build runs from each agent can show flakes from sibling agents' partial state (uncompiled new commands, missing dispatch cases, in-flight test fixture changes). Do NOT treat these as real failures while agents are running. Re-run the full suite ONCE after every agent finishes to get a clean signal. Round-2 of claude-code-profiles-03m saw 4 agents independently report 1-7 failures from the same flakes; quiescent run was 810/810 green.

When changing a 'did you mean: X' style suggestion wording, search for ALL emit sites — a project may have multiple constructors (e.g. formatDidYouMean helper + MissingProfileError.toString) that each build the suggestion string independently. Updating only the helper leaves the error-class path emitting the old wording. Verify with 'git grep "did you mean"' across src/ AND tests/ before committing.

Cloudflare Pages native GitHub integration deploys on push and posts PR previews with zero YAML — no custom deploy workflow needed, no GH Actions secrets required. Build command and output dir live in the CF dashboard. Strictly safer than a custom workflow because there's no CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN to leak via fork-PR exfil. When the user says 'CD on every main push', ask whether they specifically need the workflow file in the repo (audit trail) — if not, prefer CF native and document the dashboard config in site/README.md instead.

Starlight (v0.30+) injects its catch-all route at [...slug] and has no built-in URL prefix option. To mount Starlight under a sub-path like /docs/ while keeping a separate marketing landing at /, nest the docs collection content under src/content/docs/docs/. Astro's explicit src/pages/index.astro wins over Starlight's catch-all for /. Astro v6 also moved the content collection config from src/content/config.ts to src/content.config.ts (LegacyContentConfigError if not moved).

Astro Starlight customCss is injected BEFORE Starlight's own common.css, so plain :root { --sl-*: ... } overrides lose to Starlight's same-specificity defaults by source order. Fix: use :root:root (specificity 0,2,0) or :root:root[data-theme=...] (0,3,0) to win cascade. Verify with: grep -oE '\-\-sl-color-bg:[^;}]*' dist/_astro/{global,common}.*.css — both files declare the variable; computed style takes the higher-specificity rule. Starlight's :root defaults are the DARK theme; light mode lives at :root[data-theme=light].
